verifier_values.criteria: States the physician prefers gentamicin in this patient.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
1. The physician favors the antibiotic that does not cause QT prolongation. This information is found in the script (Script E4.pdf): "But I want to stay away from anything that prolongs the QT."

2. Gentamicin is not listed among drugs that cause QTc prolongation. 
-https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534864/
-See table 1: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3653016/

3. Ciprofloxacin is listed among drugs that cause QTc prolongation. 
-https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534864/
-See table 1: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3653016/

verifier_values.criteria: Notes the patient's most recent documented weight from the chart is 50.8 kg (acceptable range 50.3 to 51.3).
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
This information is found in the patient's chart ("Onc_Patient 7"): "112 lb (50.8 kg)"
-Clinical notes tab, row 247, Column D

verifier_values.criteria: States extended-interval dosing for gentamicin is dependent upon renal function.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
Renal function affects frequency of gentamicin dosing: "Every 24 hours for those with normal renal function and longer for those with renal dysfunction." 
https://www.uptodate.com/contents/dosing-and-administration-of-parenteral-aminoglycosides

verifier_values.criteria: States the frequency of gentamicin would be once daily in this patient.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
1. The patient has normal renal function. This information is extracted from attached lab report (Lab A.pdf): "eGFR 102 mL/min/1.73"

2. The frequency of gentamicin dosing for patients with normal renal function is once daily. 
"Extended-interval aminoglycoside therapy (also known as once-daily aminoglycosides, single daily aminoglycoside dosing, consolidated or high-dose aminoglycoside therapy) utilizes a higher weight-based dose administered at an extended interval (every 24 hours for those with normal renal function and longer for those with renal dysfunction)." -https://www.uptodate.com/contents/dosing-and-administration-of-parenteral-aminoglycosides

verifier_values.criteria: States the patient should be given a gentamicin dose of 7 mg/kg (acceptable range 6.9 to 7.1).
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
7 mg/kg is the dose referenced in the Hartford Nomogram that is recommended for the patient's pneumonia. 
--From the attached script:
"Doctor: This time is worse, we call it severe. We grade your pneumonia for its severity. Your
x-ray, your labs, your presentation. We want to prevent it from worsening, so we need to use
the right drugs."
--From the prompt:
"The patient is hospitalized for shortness of breath within a few weeks of the most recent office visit and develops severe gram-negative pneumonia. "

1. "The Hartford Nomogram method utilizes high-dose, once daily dosing to optimize the peak/MIC ratio in most clinical situations by administering a dose of 7mg/kg of either gentamicin or tobramycin." -https://med.stanford.edu/content/dam/sm/bugsanddrugs/documents/antimicrobial-dosing-protocols/SHC-Aminoglycoside-Dosing-Guide.pdf
2. "High-dose extended dosing strategy: Other infecions (exclude CF)	7 mg/kg IV q24h" -https://idmp.ucsf.edu/content/gentamicin

verifier_values.criteria: Calculates the daily dose of gentamicin for this patient as 348.5-362.7 mg.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
The dose calculation is weight x 7 mg/kg. 50.8 kg x 7 mg/kg = 355.6 mg dosed every 24 hours.
1. "The Hartford Nomogram method utilizes high-dose, once daily dosing to optimize the peak/MIC ratio in most clinical situations by administering a dose of 7mg/kg of either gentamicin or tobramycin." -https://med.stanford.edu/content/dam/sm/bugsanddrugs/documents/antimicrobial-dosing-protocols/SHC-Aminoglycoside-Dosing-Guide.pdf
2. "7 mg/kg IV q24h" -https://idmp.ucsf.edu/content/gentamicin

Answer given with a 2% margin of error.
